name: Zurich
profile: Switzerland's largest city and its financial centre — banking, insurance and reinsurance, alongside the country's deepest technology market, with research anchored by ETH Zurich and the University of Zurich. German-speaking: the language of naturalisation and permit procedures here is German. Everyday life runs largely on Swiss-German dialect, but the recognised tests (fide, telc, Goethe) are set in Standard German. Requirements and accepted proof vary by canton and commune — confirm yours with the cantonal migration authority.

name: Geneva
profile: Switzerland's international city — the European seat of the United Nations and home to many international organisations and NGOs, alongside private banking and watchmaking, with the University of Geneva. French-speaking: the language of naturalisation and permit procedures here is French, proven through fide or a recognised certificate such as DELF or TCF. Working in English for an international employer does not change the cantonal language requirement. Requirements and accepted proof vary by canton and commune — confirm yours with the cantonal migration authority.

name: Basel
profile: The centre of Switzerland's pharmaceutical and life-sciences industry, with chemicals, medical research and logistics, and the University of Basel. It sits where Switzerland, France and Germany meet and many people commute across a border, but the canton's own procedures are German-speaking, and the recognised tests are set in Standard German rather than Swiss-German dialect. Requirements and accepted proof vary by canton and commune — confirm yours with the cantonal migration authority.

name: Bern
region: Canton of Bern — German-speaking (canton officially bilingual)
profile: The seat of Switzerland's federal government — the Federal Assembly, the Federal Council and the federal administration, including the State Secretariat for Migration (SEM) — with the University of Bern. Switzerland has no capital city in law; Bern is the federal city. The city is German-speaking, but the canton is officially bilingual German and French, with a French-speaking area in the Bernese Jura, so which language applies can depend on your commune. Requirements and accepted proof vary by canton and commune — confirm yours with the cantonal migration authority.

name: Lausanne
profile: A research and education centre on Lake Geneva — EPFL and the University of Lausanne drive engineering, technology and life sciences, and the city hosts the International Olympic Committee. French-speaking: the language of naturalisation and permit procedures here is French, proven through fide or a recognised certificate such as DELF or TCF. Requirements and accepted proof vary by canton and commune — confirm yours with the cantonal migration authority.
